data.title: Okta Lost The Cybersecurity Plot (Q4 Review)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Okta (OKTA), I downgraded the stock to a "hold," as management was repeatedly failing to demonstrate meaningful signs of revenue acceleration, despite management’s optimism towards their product innovation roadmap that they believed would benefit from industry tailwinds around securing agents and non-human identities.

The company reported their Q4 FY26 earnings yesterday, and unfortunately, management failed to deliver once again.

While they beat their revenue and non-GAAP operating margin guidance for the quarter, forward guidance shows no sign of inflection.

On the contrary.

Management now expects forward revenues to slow compared to their FY26 levels with a slight deterioration in margins.

This is not what "benefiting from industry tailwinds" looks like.

With no improvement in their Net Retention Rate for four consecutive quarters now, I believe that the company continues to face sluggish growth in new customer additions, upsells, and renewals and is thus slowly fading into irrelevance.

The only way out of this misery would be a potential takeover, especially as it has solid product leadership in Identity and Access Management, along with a strong balance sheet.

Unfortunately, with its decelerating revenue momentum along with its past history of security incidents, potential buyers may be hesitant.

Until then, we can expect the stock to remain pressured, and I will therefore downgrade it to a "sell."

Better-than-expected Performance for Both Top and Bottom Line

Okta just reported its Q4 FY26 earnings, where both revenue and cRPO (Current Remaining Performance Obligations) continued to slow from the previous quarter, growing 11% and 12% YoY, vs. management’s guidance of 10% and 9%, respectively.

The outperformance in results compared to management’s guidance was likely driven by their $100K+ ACV (Annual Contract Value) customers, which continued to grow more or less at a steady pace compared to the previous quarter, now representing over 80% of total ACV. At the same time, their total base of $1M+ ACV grew at an accelerated pace of 16% to 545 customers, which now represents $1B in total ACV.

In terms of their platform contribution, Workforce Identity ACV grew 10% YoY, representing 59% of total ACV, while Customer Identity ACV grew 12% YoY, contributing the remaining 41% of total ACV. As I have explained in my previous posts, Okta Workforce is directed towards large enterprises and IT teams with use cases that include employee access, workflows, and compliance across cloud and on-premise applications. Meanwhile, their Customer Identity platform is directed towards developers and product teams.

To reiterate, Okta offers two primary platforms that include Okta Workforce and Okta Customer Identity, which are powered by Auth0 technology. Okta Workforce is directed towards large enterprises and IT teams with use cases that include employee access, workflows, and compliance across cloud and on-premises applications. Meanwhile, the Okta Customer Identity platform powered by Auth0 is directed towards developers and product teams that are building user-facing/customer applications that require a secure and customizable authentication and authorization experience for their users who are accessing the service. Last quarter, they also launched Auth0 for AI agents to help their customers build secure AI agents across their B2B, B2C, and internal app ecosystems.

As Okta doubles down on their product innovation roadmap, what is interesting to note is that their margins haven’t taken a beating. During the quarter, Okta continued to expand their total gross margin to 82.1%. The same is true for their non-GAAP operating margins, which continued to expand both on a sequential and year-over-year basis to 26.5%, 150 basis points higher than what management had guided for.

No Inflection in Sight

However, forward guidance continues to remain lackluster, with revenue and cRPO projected to slow to 9% and 10%, respectively, in Q1 FY27. For the full year FY27, management expects revenue to grow just 9% YoY to $3.18B (at the midpoint), which would mark a deceleration on a YoY basis.

We can also see this playing out in the Net Retention figures, which have now remained steady at 106% for the last four consecutive quarters. With no signs of an inflection higher, this indicates that the pace of new customer additions, upsells, and/or renewals continues to remain sluggish.

On top of that, Okta seems to be reaching a plateau with its operating leverage, especially as revenue growth fails to pick up momentum. For the full year FY27, management is projecting non-GAAP operating margins at 25-26%, compared to 26% in FY26, while free cash flow margin is also expected to head lower in the 27-28% range, compared to 30% in FY26.

What Can Halt Its Descent to Irrelevance

Looking forward, consensus estimates are pointing to Okta growing in the high single-digit range over the next three years to roughly $3.75B by FY29. From a profitability standpoint, its earnings per share are expected to grow at a slightly faster rate than revenue growth expectations, with analysts expecting roughly $4.65 in earnings per share by FY29.

The stock is currently trading at a forward non-GAAP PE ratio of 20x, which is expected to further compress to 16.3x when taking FY29 earnings per share estimates into account.

Sure, the stock is “cheap” by traditional valuation methods. But, then again, with enterprises increasingly consolidating towards fully integrated cybersecurity platforms, the inflection would be hard to come by for Okta at this stage. Without a meaningful inflection in their revenue growth, the stock will just slowly fade into irrelevance, which is already happening at the moment.

What can save Okta at this point?

A potential takeover perhaps.

We saw that with Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW) acquiring Okta’s nearest competitor CyberArk to deepen their platformization strategy. But then again, CyberArk had been growing its revenues at a significantly faster pace than Okta’s.

When it comes to Okta, it has several characteristics of a potential takeover candidate. First, its product leadership in Identity and Access Management is strong, along with a rock-solid balance sheet. Not to mention, its forward PE ratio is at its lowest level in years and below industry averages.

However, its decelerating growth, along with past security incidents, may be keeping potential buyers at bay for now.

My Final Verdict and Conclusions

With Okta failing to show any signs of revenue inflection despite its product innovation and Agentic AI as a tailwind, the company continues to fade into irrelevance.

The situation is probably getting further aggravated amid ongoing vendor consolidation as enterprises move away from point solutions towards fully integrated platforms to securely deploy AI at scale.

Therefore, without any compelling event, such as a potential takeover, investor sentiment is unlikely to improve on Okta even though the company has a rock-solid balance sheet and is trading at its lowest PE ratio in over a year.

data.title: Oil Spike May Scramble The Fed's Policy Script As War In Iran Continues
data.description:
By James Picerno

The Federal Reserve was already facing a delicate transition as Kevin Warsh prepares to take over in May, pending Senate confirmation, when Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s term ends. The war in Iran adds another layer of uncertainty to an already fraught policy environment.

Energy prices have spiked since the US and Israel attacked Iran on Saturday, a conflict that’s slowed oil exports to a crawl through the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly one-fifth of the world’s seaborne oil trade moved through this strategic chokepoint last year. If this narrow waterway remains closed for an extended period, it raises the possibility that inflation could heat up and force the Fed to respond - if not by raising interest rates, then by delaying rate cuts for longer than recently expected.

Fed funds futures are now downplaying the odds of a rate cut in June. Before the war, this was considered the month when the central bank would start cutting again, extending last year’s run of easing. But the market is rethinking that logic and is now pricing in a moderately high probability that the Fed leaves rates steady in June, pushing out the expected date for the next cut to July or possibly September at the earliest.

The concern is that if energy costs remain elevated, the higher prices of crude oil, gasoline, and natural gas will soon feed through to the broader economy, delivering a so-called negative supply shock. Standard economic theory, along with the historical record, suggests that the higher prices will slow growth and lift inflation to some degree.

Energy prices are expected to fall once the conflict is over, but US and Israeli officials say the war could run for weeks. A related concern is that the widening damage to energy infrastructure in the Persian Gulf region from Iran’s retaliatory attacks will take time to repair - a bottleneck that will keep supplies lower for longer.

Geopolitics vs. monetary policy

The main concern for the Fed is how, or if, to respond to higher energy costs. To the extent that oil drives headline inflation higher, the shift presents an especially difficult challenge for monetary policy, since interest rates have no direct effect on the supply of crude. That’s one reason why the Fed and other central banks tend to target core measures of inflation, which strip out energy and food costs - a subset of pricing over which policy has substantial influence.

The Fed’s job will be even tougher because deciding if the war has raised inflation in a meaningful and persistent degree will take time. Next week’s scheduled release of consumer price inflation for February, for instance, is already irrelevant in the current environment.

TMC Research’s Fed Model continues to indicate that policy remains modestly tight, but the hawkish spread of the effective Fed funds rate over our estimated neutral rate is fading. As recently as late January, the Fed’s target rate was more than 60 basis points above this proxy for estimating an equilibrium level. The premium (implying a moderately hawkish bias) has been sliding since, dropping to under 30 basis points on Tuesday (March 3). In other words, Fed policy is approaching a neutral bias, and looks set to continue into dovish terrain - a trend that may quickly become inappropriate if inflation heats up.

Hikes Off the Table - For Now

Rate hikes are still unlikely, based on market expectations for inflation. The Treasury market’s 5-year breakeven inflation rate (nominal less inflation-indexed yield) was just below 2.5% yesterday (March 4), a middling level versus recent history for this bond market inflation gauge.

The 1-month Treasury yield appears to be pricing in no change in the target rate, as it remains at the upper half of the policy channel. The calculus would change if the 1-month yield - currently at 3.75% - decisively breaks above the channel’s upper range, marked in the chart below by the Fed’s discount window primary rate, which is also at 3.75%. In that case, the shift would signal that the market is starting to factor in the possibility of a rate hike.

This macro mix would be hard enough for any new Fed chairman, but it may be especially challenging for Warsh, who has supported President Trump’s public calls for lower interest rates, partly on the view that an artificial intelligence-driven productivity boom will boost economic growth without raising inflation.

Whatever the merits of that forecast (and there’s ongoing debate on the topic), the Iran war may rewrite the script for Warsh’s policy plans well before he arrives at the Eccles Building on Constitution Avenue.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: B2Gold: Strong Growth Is Overshadowed By The Bearish Technical Outlook
data.description:
I'm providing a post-earnings update for B2Gold (BTG) for Q4 2025. BTG reported strong revenue growth as the gross margin continued to grow. The stock is still trading at a bargain valuation. The company's expected growth for 2026 looks strong. My only concern is that the stock has formed a bearish technical set-up, which typically results in a stock correction.

B2Gold has been a standout performing stock as it is up 19% since my last update was written in September 2025. The stock is up 62% since I first initiated coverage on B2Gold in July 2025. I expect the stock to continue to perform well in 2026 if the underlying price of gold and the sentiment towards the industry remains strong. However, a shorter-term correction looks likely for BTG's stock and the underlying price of gold.

Insights From the Q4 2025 Earnings Report

The Q4 2025 earnings report provides multiple insights into B2Gold's likely growth for 2026.

The company is forecasting to produce 820,000 to 970,000 ounces in 2026. The projection is lower for 2026 due to the planned step-down of Otjikoto after the completion of the open pit mining in Q4 2025. It is also due to lower production at the Fekola mine due to the continuation of the stripping of Phase 8 in the Fekola pit. The lower production is expected to be partially offset by the continued ramp-up of the Goose mine. BTG stated that all of their operations are performing above expectations so far in 2026.

One key possible growth driver for 2026 is that B2Gold expects to obtain the permit from Mali for Fekola regional as early as Q1 with production beginning in the 2nd half of 2026. BTG is projecting Fekola Regional to produce 60,000 to 80,000 ounces in 2026. Obtaining the permit for Fekola can be a long-term growth driver for the company in 2026 and beyond.

The company expects the Goose mine to ramp up throughout the year. Expectations are for Goose's crushing circuit to produce 3,200 tonnes per day after some modifications to improve performance. BTG is studying improvements to get the crushing circuit up to an average of 4,000 tonnes per day. Those studies are expected to be finalized in the 1st half of 2026.

BTG expects another year of consistent operations at Masbate in 2026. This follows production at Masbate that exceeded the company's expectations with 2 years in a row of record results in 2024 & 2025. BTG expects Otjikoto to produce less in 2026 as the mine transitions to processing only the Wolfshag underground ore, supplemented by low-grade stockpiles. The company is developing the Antelope deposit, which BTG believes can get Otjikoto to produce an average of 110,000 ounces from 2029 to 2032.

B2Gold continues to return capital to shareholders in the form of stock repurchases. BTG repurchased 2 million shares worth $10 million in 2025. The company also repurchased another 5 million shares worth about $24 million so far in 2026. BTG plans to continue the repurchases for the remainder of 2026. These repurchases reduce the amount of shares outstanding, making existing shares more valuable for stockholders.

Overall, B2Gold is displaying multiple solid potential growth drivers. These growth drivers should help the company to continue to grow revenue and earnings at a strong pace in 2026.

Technical Perspective

The main concern that I have for B2Gold is its bearish technical set-up.

B2Gold's daily chart above shows the stock in a bearish divergence. This is indicated as the stock price made a higher high from the end of January to the end of February. At the same time, the RSI made a lower high. The last time a bearish divergence like this occurred was in October. The October divergence resulted in the stock dropping from just below $6 down to $3.70.

I don't know if the stock will decline by such a drastic amount this time. I do think that the stock could experience a significant profit-taking pullback with this bearish-looking set-up. A pullback recently began over the past few days.

The MACD just made a bearish move. This is indicated as the blue MACD line crossed below the red signal line while the histogram bars turned from green to red. This suggests that the momentum shifted from positive to negative. This momentum shift could lead to a longer decline for BTG's stock.

It is important to note that any pullback in the stock would probably be shallow if the price of gold remains strong. B2Gold's stock tends to correlate well with the underlying price of gold.

The chart above displays the monthly chart of the underlying price of gold. B2Gold's stock tends to correlate well with the price of gold. So, it is important to understand where the price of the commodity is likely to go.

The price of gold reached an extreme overbought level on the monthly chart as indicated in the mid-90s on the RSI. Levels this high on the RSI are typically unsustainable. The price recently began to decline so far in March as the RSI dropped from the mid-90s to the low 90s.

The MACD is indicating waning momentum so far in March as the histogram bars turned from dark green to light green. This could be the beginning of a larger correction for the price of gold. If this occurs, it would likely negatively impact BTG's stock.

Valuation

B2Gold is still trading at a bargain valuation. The stock is trading at 7.5x expected EPS of $0.73 for 2026. This is significantly below the sector median forward PE of 16x. I also like to use the PEG ratio for growth companies like BTG, as it takes multiple years of growth into account. BTG has a low PEG ratio of 0.56. This is also below the sector median PEG of 1.2. The growth stocks that I cover tend to perform well when the PEG is below two. So, when I see a strong growth stock with a PEG below one, that really shows a bargain.

The expected EPS of $0.73 for 2026 would be about 60% growth over 2025. This can be achieved with analysts' expected revenue growth of 37% to $4.2 billion for 2026. Then, earnings can get a boost from the margin expansion that BTG has been achieving. The gross margin increased from 56.5% in 2024 to 64.4% in 2025. The operating margin increased from 30% to 36.8%, and the net income margin increased from a negative (33%) to 13% over the same time.

B2Gold's low valuation leaves plenty of room to the upside for the stock due to the potential for PE expansion. At this low level, the stock has a good chance to increase at a rate that exceeds BTG's earnings growth.

Risks for the Stock

Since B2Gold's stock is highly correlated to the price of gold, sharp drops in the price of gold would likely result in sharp declines in BTG's stock. Lower gold prices would lead to lower revenue for BTG, as the company would earn less from each ounce of gold sold. The price of gold is currently at extreme overbought levels, which may not be sustainable in the near term. This could lead to a sharp correction for the price of gold and B2Gold's stock.

An unexpected delay in obtaining the Fekola Regional permit from Mali could lead to a sell-off for the stock. A long delay would lower BTG's production estimates for Fekola Regional for 2026, leading to less revenue & earnings for the year.

B2Gold faces geopolitical, legal, and regulatory risks in the countries where it operates. This includes Mali, Canada, Namibia, the Philippines, and Colombia. These countries could impose new laws and regulations, which could increase BTG's costs of doing business or make it more difficult to expand production.

The recent military actions between the U.S./Israel, and Iran could trigger a sell-off for the broader market which could negatively impact B2Gold.

B2Gold's Investment Outlook

B2Gold looks great from a growth perspective through at least 2026. The ramp-up of the Goose mine and obtaining the permit at Fekola Regional can be strong growth drivers this year. The stock's valuation remains attractive, which, along with BTG's strong earnings growth, has the potential to help drive the stock for strong above-average long-term gains.

B2Gold does face some short-term uncertainties. The military actions between the U.S./Israel and Iran could lead to a sell-off for the market and BTG's stock. Even without this event occurring, the stock's technical level suggests the potential for a profit-taking pullback. Investors should watch to see how the market reacts to this recent news to re-evaluate their positions. I'm lowering my rating for the stock to 'HOLD' from 'STRONG BUY' due to the technical stock level and the uncertainties regarding the new geopolitical situation.

data.title: Software's Big Sell-Off: Structural Risk Or Narrative Noise?
data.description:
By Lei Qiu, John H. Fogarty, CFA, Kent Hargis, PhD and Thorsten Winkelmann

AI concerns are forcing a rapid reset of software stocks. But disruption rarely unfolds as fast as markets expect.

Software stocks have suffered a rude awakening amid fears that artificial intelligence (AI) will upend the industry. While the outlook is highly uncertain, the indiscriminate market response may be conflating structurally exposed software businesses with more durable companies.

That distinction defines the challenge for investors. AI is raising real doubts about software business models that cannot be ignored. But not all software firms are alike. Some will buckle, while others will survive—and may even thrive—in an AI-driven world.

We believe the market is pricing in disruption as if it’s universal and immediate. Yet technology regime shifts are never so simple. The task now is to identify where disruption is likely to reshape economics and where fears may be running ahead of fundamentals.

Could AI Agents Redefine an Industry?

Since ChatGPT launched, software stocks have been under scrutiny. In recent months, the release of AI coding models Claude Opus 4.5 and Claude Cowork reinforced fears that increasingly capable AI agents—which are being massively deployed (Display)—could commoditize software by reducing development costs and lowering barriers to entry. The MSCI World Software & Services Index tumbled by more than 20% this year through the end of February.

The market is signaling that every software company is at risk—a dramatic change for an industry that has enjoyed positive sentiment for years. Investors have lost conviction in the durability of economic characteristics that historically defined high-quality software businesses, making it nearly impossible to forecast the terminal value of software shares.

The Bear Case: Disruption Could Hit Profit Pools

Profitability concerns are hard to disprove. If AI agents can autonomously execute complex workflows, customers may no longer need full software applications. As a result, traditional user interfaces would lose relevance while switching costs fall and pricing power erodes. More broadly, if AI lowers barriers to entry across large portions of the software ecosystem, supply will rise faster than demand. Increased competition would pressure pricing, margins and returns on capital for incumbents—especially those relying on seat based or feature-driven pricing models.

These risks cannot be dismissed. That said, truly autonomous agents capable of managing complex, regulated, enterprise-scale workflows remain limited today to areas like coding assistance and customer service. So the long-term trajectory is uncertain, and the timing of economic impact remains unclear.

Not All Firms Face the Same Risks

Disruptive impacts won’t be distributed equally across the broad software industry, in our view. Companies providing horizontal application layers—such as customer relations management or workflow automation—are very different to vertical providers of banking systems or retail sales systems, to name a few. Business dynamics differ for infrastructure and cybersecurity firms.

So the key question is: How easy or hard is it to replace certain types of software? Systems of record, for example, aren’t easily displaced because these mission-critical platforms are deeply embedded in organizations with authoritative data sets and compliance frameworks. In contrast, interface apps with low switching costs and limited proprietary data are much easier to replace.

Is the Bear Narrative Running Ahead of Financial Reality?

Nobody really knows where value will accrue across the AI stack (applications, agents, data or infrastructure). This explains why the market has pushed up the discount rate applied to software stocks across the board, which has dragged down valuations (Display). Share prices have fallen despite little sign of operational stress in customer behavior, retention metrics or reported financials.

That said, today’s benign fundamentals may be misleading. Since AI disruption is a structural risk, it wouldn’t show in current company metrics. Firms with solid margins and revenue growth today might look healthy even though pricing and competitive threats loom large.

Another unknown: How will competition intensify among software incumbents as advances in AI coding lower the cost and time required to expand into adjacent categories? This will blur the lines between traditional software domains and erode natural barriers that protect legacy positions.

Financial Transparency Matters: Mind the GAAP

In the search for evidence of AI-driven weakness, accounting transparency will be prized. We expect the market to have less tolerance for non-generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) metrics that adjust earnings by removing stock-based compensation costs.

Structural disruption concerns will compel investors to place greater emphasis on near-term GAAP profitability and traditional valuation metrics. Although GAAP status didn’t protect software stocks in the recent sell-off, we think margin discipline and earnings quality will matter more over time.

Three Things to Watch

The software industry is no stranger to disruption. Well before AI, software was repeatedly redefined by the internet, cloud computing and software as a service (SaaS). To separate winners from losers as AI progresses, investors should focus a playbook on:

  1. Durable competitive moats: Mission-critical software that delivers clear economic value should still command pricing power in a more competitive landscape. Software companies that own authoritative enterprise datasets will be better placed to defend their competitive advantages. And customers will be more reluctant to pay high costs to replace software that is horizontally integrated across an organization.
  2. Clear signs of monetizing AI activity: Software firms must show that AI is helping grow their revenue. Some platforms will benefit if AI agents boost transaction volume (more tickets, approvals and workflows), so look for evidence that AI is bolstering core platforms rather than disintermediating them. In contrast, business models that rely on static seat pricing look vulnerable.
  3. Earnings quality and business model resilience: Businesses with high gross customer retention rates and products embedded in core workflows can defend earnings. Coherent AI strategies have become table stakes for software companies, and proven GAAP-based profitability profiles—which are hard to find right now—will be essential. Distressed valuations could open opportunities in resilient companies that have been tarred with the same brush in the indiscriminate sell-off.

At this juncture in the AI-software story, there’s good reason to be cautious. Structural risk to the software sector is real. History shows that transformative technological disruption takes time to play out, but the magnitude of its impact is often greater than initially expected.

With this in mind, we believe investors shouldn’t be guided by doomsday scenarios or near-term volatility.

In volatile markets, investors tend to shoot first and ask questions later. But that’s not a long-term strategy. As we see it, equity portfolio teams rooted in deep research must ask questions every day to determine where the narrative noise is overdone and how to position appropriately in a software industry facing major disruptive forces. By actively assessing industry developments and the impact on individual companies, disciplined investors can balance patience with conviction while carefully adding exposure in select software stocks that look overly sold and may be primed for recovery.

The authors wish to thank Senior Research Analysts Jay Pilkerton, Pradeep Ramani and Jim Russo for their invaluable contributions to this blog.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Municipal Income Fund is a diversified national municipal bond strategy investing in general obligation and revenue-backed municipal securities across the yield curve.
  • Our investment approach focuses on fundamental credit analysis, yield-curve positioning and an analysis of the structural characteristics of each security.
  • The fund's interest rate sensitivity is targeted closely to that of its benchmark to prevent interest rate speculation from overwhelming research-based strategies that we deem to have a higher likelihood of long-term success.
  • We emphasize a total-return approach that seeks to generate a high level of tax-exempt income, consistent with the preservation of capital.

All or a portion of the fund's income may be subject to the federal alternative minimum tax. Income or fund distributions attributable to capital gains are usually subject to both state and federal income taxes.

Municipal Market Review

Tax-exempt municipal bonds capped off 2025 with a resilient fourth quarter, sustaining the momentum they gained in the prior three months as interest rates declined, tax-backed credit fundamentals remained solid, and demand ticked higher. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index gained 1.56% (including interest payments and price changes) for the quarter, helping munis offset a volatile first half of 2025 and lifting their full-year advance to 4.25%.

Munis notched most of their Q4 gain in October (+1.24%). That month, the U.S. Federal Reserve reduced its benchmark policy interest rate by a quarter percentage point (25 basis points), marking the central bank's second such rate cut in as many months, aimed at supporting a softening job market.

Improving technical factors were an additional tailwind. On the supply side, the pace of new bond issuance decelerated. Meanwhile, demand for munis strengthened, particularly among investors looking to lock in yields ahead of further anticipated interest-rate cuts. Moreover, passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in July preserved the tax-exempt status of municipal bonds, removing a headwind that had created uncertainty in the muni market during the first half of 2025.

Munis then largely held steady in November (+0.23%) and December (+0.09%), supported by another quarter-point Federal Reserve rate reduction in December. Although new-issue volume remained elevated and contributed to a record year for issuance, it slowed toward year-end amid the typical seasonal lull in new deals. Supply was largely absorbed without market disruption amid firming investor demand, as healthy yields continued to attract buyers' attention.

Credit fundamentals for most tax-backed munis remained solid throughout the three months, with default rates very low and issuers' balance sheets generally healthy.

For the full quarter, long-duration municipals led the market, with 10-, 15-, 20- and 22-plus-year bonds posting the top gains (+1.74%, +2.84%, +2.20% and 1.63%, respectively), while one-, three-, five- and seven-year munis advanced 0.56%, 0.46%, 0.50% and 0.83%, respectively.

From a sector perspective, revenue bonds (+1.57%) performed roughly in line with general obligation bonds (+1.60%). Among GOs, local-government debt (+1.71%) modestly outpaced state-backed bonds (+1.44%). In the revenue category, there was little differentiation between the performance of various segments or ratings. Housing (+1.77%) and hospital bonds (+1.75%) fared best, while most other sectors followed closely behind – except for industrial development bonds, which returned only 0.65%.

By credit-quality rating, BBB-rated bonds gained 1.77%, slightly bettering returns in the 1.50% range notched by munis with higher ratings (AAA, AA, A).

data.title: The MacBook Neo Is A Brilliant Move For Apple, But A Worrying Sign For The Economy
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL)) is pushing hard into the budget computer market with the new MacBook Neo, which uses an iPhone chip and costs just $599 ($499 with an education discount). Given how many casual computer users there are these days, this move strikes me as a smart step and one that could help bolster Apple’s presence not just in the PC market, but potentially the education market as well. I’ve cooled on Apple as an investment in recent years because the company has, in some ways, stagnated, offering few major new products. Tech-wise, the Neo isn’t groundbreaking, but economics-wise, it should prove to be a big winner.

That said, I also believe the MacBook Neo hints at the increasingly ossified K-shaped economy. Until now, I don't believe Apple has ever directly sold a laptop for less than $999 in terms of base price. That said, you could find generous sales via third parties, use education discounts, or buy the aging M1 MacBook through Walmart (WMT) for less than $700 (I believe this is no longer offered).

I suspect the Neo is a direct response to rising economic inequality and financially pressured consumers. It's important for investors to mitigate associated risks, but AAPL actually offers a good way to do so, as I'll explain below.

Apple Just Massively Expanded Its Potential Customer Base

First, let's size up the budget goliaths. Alphabet’s (GOOG) Chromebooks dominate the education market, controlling a whopping 93% of the U.S. education market and more than 60% of the global education market. Chromebooks typically sell for considerably less than a comparable (spec-wise) Windows or Mac laptop, which goes a long way in explaining the dominance in schools. The Neo is still more expensive than the average Chromebook, but it closes the gap by quite a bit. It’ll be interesting to see if Apple will try to make a strong push to get Macs into schools.

Of course, schools aren’t the only way to enlarge Apple’s customer base. Many casual computer users simply need something for basic functions, like cruising the web, publishing analyses on Seeking Alpha, and checking e-mail. The Neo seems to be targeting these users in particular. While iPads have been around for years and often cost less than $600, the form factor and iPadOS come with a fair number of limitations.

The more affordable price will likely prove especially tempting for people who travel a lot. It’s easy to misplace your laptop when traveling, or you might get caught in the rain and your computer may suffer water damage. The Neo is a rather small laptop, which makes it great for life on the go. The battery life is solid with the Neo getting 10 hours plus while surfing the web. Still, this lags the MacBook Air, which gets 15 hours per charge.

Perhaps the biggest opportunity to grow is in international and especially emerging markets. I’ve traveled through and lived in emerging markets. Interestingly, Windows computers are often much more expensive than they are in the United States. Macs generally have pretty uniform pricing globally. Anyway, a $1000 laptop is quite expensive for someone earning $10,000 or less per year. The math with the Neo might still keep the model out of reach of many, but it’s a big step in the right direction.

Apple Pushing Value Could Hint At Economic Malaise

Traditionally, Apple has shied away from budget offerings when it comes to MacBooks and computers in general. Budget iPhones and iPads are available, but by and large, Apple protects its brand and prestige by focusing on high-quality products and avoiding frequent sales and discounts. Apple has and will work with other parties to offer budget computers. Walmart, for example, was selling brand new M1 MacBook Airs for between $699 to $599. This allowed Apple to create a channel to sell budget laptops without having to do it directly, as that could have damaged their brand.

In the K-shape economy, consumers in high-income brackets are still spending at a fast rate. The middle class and below have been struggling. I suspect part of the reason why Apple is entering the budget market is because of the K-shape economy. Value is king right now. In the restaurant sector, those companies, like Chipotle (CMG), that don’t emphasize value have been struggling. McDonald’s (MCD) also had some rough quarters after frequent price increases chased away customers. Once McDonald’s started to emphasize value meals, its fortunes improved.

That so many companies are having to push deals and value so hard hints at underlying problems in the economy. So far, consumer spending has been holding steady, but if our current trajectory doesn’t change, eventually the bottom will fall out. How should investors react to the current market? Finding companies that emphasize value, such as McDonald’s and now even Apple, is prudent. Ditto for companies that target wealthier customers, which also includes Apple.

AAPL Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

While Apple was once the world’s largest company in terms of market cap, it now lags behind Nvidia (NVDA), and Google is hot on its heels. Apple’s ~33 P/E ratio (TTM) is high compared to the overall market, but not especially high for a Magnificent 7 stock. Ditto for the 8.96 P/S ratio (TTM). Looking at Seeking Alpha’s Quant tools, as shown below, Apple gets an F score. I think historically that’s fair, but in the current expensive market, it’s pretty reasonable.

Growth also gets poor marks. YOY revenue growth weighs in at 10%. On its own, this growth strikes me as quite solid for such a mature company. Further, as shown below, revenue growth is on the upswing. However, I’m not sure if Apple would be able to maintain improving revenue growth in the current market.

Apple excels in profitability. Despite being hardware-focused, AAPL is able to maintain high net income margins (~27%). By and large, that profitability has been trending up, as illustrated below, but it did flatten out a little bit. Whether it’s a temporary hiccup or the sign of more serious economic pains, I couldn’t say.

Currently, AAPL has $66.9 billion in cash and short-term investments, as shown below. This war chest should help if AAPL decides to embark on a major pivot, say joining the AI race. Apple has a lot of current liabilities, however, totaling ~$162.4. billion. That said, I’m not worried about Apple’s financial health.

Seeking Alpha’s quant tools give AAPL a Hold rating, with valuation and growth getting low marks. Among Seeking Alpha analysts, Buy ratings actually are the most common. Sell ratings, too, are common, while somewhat ironically, Hold ratings are quite rare. Ultimately, the shift towards vale strikes me as a prudent move. That, combined with solid fundamentals, has me assigning AAPL a Buy rating.

Why I Could Be Wrong

$600 is still rather expensive for a lot of people, especially in emerging markets. iPads and Chromebooks can be had for substantially less than $500. The Neo might also cannibalize the sales of the MacBook Air models. Further, other factors not considered here could weigh Apple down. For example, Apple seems to be pretty far behind in the AI race. If AI does turn out to be as valuable as many expect, it could change the calculus here.

Key Takeaway: The Neo Value Offering Merits A Buy

I wouldn’t be surprised if I someday own a MacBook Neo. For now, I may have to settle for the stock. Overall, Apple is in a solid position. Hopefully, growth picks up and profitability remains elevated. I am worried about the state of the consumer and the overall high dependency on either value or top earners, but I see no reason to pull out of the market yet.

data.title: John Hancock Disciplined Value Mid Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The Russell Midcap Value Index—the fund’s benchmark—returned 1.42% in the fourth quarter and 11.05% in 2025.
  • The fund underperformed the benchmark in the quarter, but it slightly outperformed over the full year.
  • Stock selection was the primary detractor from relative performance in the quarter.

Market review and outlook

U.S. equities gained ground in the final three months of 2025. The combination of better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings, and two quarter-point interest rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) helped propel the market higher. While mid-cap value stocks participated in the rally, they lagged the broader market at a time in which investors displayed a persistent preference for faster-growing companies and more speculative market segments.

Although the fund encountered headwinds in the quarter, we are pleased to have outperformed for the full year in a potentially challenging environment. We believe the positive showing stems from our “Three Circle” approach, which focuses on fundamentals, valuations, and business momentum. Our process helped us identify compelling bottom-up opportunities and overcome the challenges posed by the robust returns for lower-quality stocks and companies related to high-growth themes such as AI and bitcoin.

Contributors and detractors

The fund’s fourth quarter underperformance, which largely reflected a sizable shortfall in December, was primarily the result of the stocks it didn’t own. For example, the lack of a position in Warner Brothers Discovery, Inc. (WBD)—which surged after it became the subject of a bidding war between two other media companies—was a key detractor from relative performance. Zero weightings in several AI-related stocks in the IT sector also played a role in the deficit. Underperformance for Zebra Technologies, Inc. (ZBRA) and CDW Corp. (CDW), both of which were affected by tariff headwinds and their lack of AI exposure, further pressured results in technology.

On the other hand, positions in Seagate Technology Holdings PLC (STX) and Keysight Technologies, Inc. (KEYS) were top contributors in both the sector and the portfolio as a whole. We further benefited from a position in EPAM Systems, Inc. (EPAM) The stock had lagged for most of 2025 on concerns its business would be hurt by AI, but it rebounded late in the year after reporting strong results and boosting its guidance.

Industrials proved to be another area of relative weakness. Although the sector was home to Expeditors International of Washington, Inc. (EXPD), one of the top individual contributors for the quarter, underperformance for Generac Holdings, Inc. (GNRC) and Resideo Technologies, Inc. (REZI), among others, offset the benefit. The fund also underperformed in consumer discretionary due to a downturn in AutoZone, Inc. (AZO).

We produced the strongest relative performance in financials, led by the asset-management firm Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. (AMG) Zero weightings in certain bitcoin-related stocks, which gave back ground following their outperformance in the first nine months of the year, were a further plus.

Portfolio changes

We continued to add to the fund’s weighting in healthcare. The portfolio was overweight in the sector at mid year, but we added a number of new holdings and finished December with an overweight. We identified improving business momentum across several subsectors, creating a broad range of attractive bottom-up opportunities. On the other hand, we reduced the fund’s allocation to industrials as valuations increased. The fund remained overweight in the sector at year-end, however. We reduced the fund’s position in information technology, mostly through sales of AI- and data center-related companies where valuations appeared rich, but we maintained an overweight in the sector. The portfolio was also overweight in consumer discretionary, while notable underweights included utilities, real estate, and materials.

data.title: I'm Buying Up To 12% Yield For Recurring Income
data.description:
Now is a great time to be an income investor, especially as the market remains volatile. This has resulted in a number of beaten down names. That's why I remain focused on businesses that generate predictable cash flows. Even better is if cash flows are backed by hard assets and/or are contracted with conservative underwriting standards.

This brings me to the following 2 picks, both of which are trading in bargain territory, with yields ranging from 6% to 12%. This could make plenty of sense for investors who wish to generate cash flow while getting a solid margin of safety, which are keys to sleeping well at night. Let's get started on what makes each a compelling high-yielding 'Buy' at present!

#1: Morgan Stanley Direct Lending - 12% Yield

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL) is an externally managed BDC that focuses on first-lien debt investments to middle market companies. It has a fairly defensive portfolio, with just 20% exposure to Software. As shown below, its Insurance Services, IT Services, and Commercial Services are its next largest segments.

MSDL is also diversified by borrowers, with no position representing over 2% of portfolio total. The borrower base is comprised of 227 portfolio companies with an average position size of just 40 bps. Moreover, MSDL's portfolio is mostly comprised of first lien debt investments, representing 96% of total investments.

MSDL benefits from its association with its 'big brother' Morgan Stanley (MS). This is reflected by 76% of its deals having multiple MS touchpoints. As shown below, MSIM has $1.9 trillion of total AUM and has a 40-year track record of alternative assets management.

MSDL has a portfolio size of $3.8 billion and generated NII per share of $0.49 during Q4 2025. This was down by $0.01 sequentially and down by $0.07 YoY due to lower interest rates. NAV per share declined by a modest $0.15 sequentially, due primarily to the restructuring and sale of one of its portfolio holdings. Nearly 70% of non-refinancing activity during Q4 was driven by new platforms, demonstrating healthy sponsor engagement.

MSDL's NAV per share has ebbed and flowed over the past 5 years within the $19.80 to $20.80 range, as shown below. Non-accruals remain low at just 1.6% of portfolio cost. Plus, PIK (payment in kind) income, represents just 3.9% of total investment income.

The market seems to be overly rotated on MSDL's software exposure, given the share price performance in recent weeks. This is considering the fact that MSDL's software exposure is modest at 20%. It also has strong protections with an average loan-to-value ratio of 40%, implying 60% equity buffer. Plus, borrower interest coverage ratios actually improved over the past year.

Moreover, MSDL is seeing a rebound in deal activity, as reflected by heighted private equity deal momentum in the second half of last year. This is supported by a sound balance sheet with a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.2x, sitting well below the 2.0 statutory limit for BDCs.

Importantly, the current reset dividend rate of $0.45 is well covered by NII/share of $0.49. MSDL also trades at a substantial discount to its NAV at the current price of $14.90. This equates to a P/NAV ratio of 0.74x, sitting near its all time low since inception, as shown below.

At the current price, I believe the market is way too pessimistic around the stock. This is considering its safe loan-to-value ratio, modest software exposure and well-covered dividend. As such, MSDL could deliver compelling total return based on the 12% dividend yield and material discount from NAV.

#2: Four Corners Property Trust - 6% Yield

Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT) is a net lease REIT that focuses on restaurant and service-oriented retail properties across the U.S. It leases to creditworthy tenants under long-term triple-net leases in which the tenant is responsible for paying property taxes, maintenance, and insurance.

At present, FCPT has 1,325 leases that are spread across 179 brands. Its portfolio is 99.6% occupied with strong tenant EBITDAR-to-rent coverage of 5.1x. Plus, 53% of FCPT's ABR (annual base rent) comes from investment grade rated tenants.

Since being spun-off from Darden Restaurants (DRI), FCPT has greatly diversified its portfolio. As shown below, legacy DRI assets leased to Olive Garden and Longhorn Steakhouse now fall well under 50% of portfolio rent.

FCPT demonstrated steady performance during Q4 2025, with AFFO per share rising by 2.2% and 2.9% YoY for the quarter and the full year, respectively. This was driven by annual rent escalators and portfolio growth. FCPT's tenants are also doing well, with Chili's reporting 9% same-store sales growth, and Olive Garden and Longhorn seeing 5% and 6% YoY sales growth, respectively.

Last year, FCPT also acquired 105 properties for $318 million at an attractive 6.8% blended cap rate. Most acquisitions were one-off properties in the $5 to $20 million range. This lessens the risk of overpaying for large portfolio transactions.

Looking ahead, FCPT has continued opportunities to diversify its portfolio, as management is looking into grocery stores, equipment rental properties, automotive services, and medical retail. These are segments that are recession and e-commerce resilient and tend to perform well even during economic downturns.

FCPT is supported by a solid balance sheet with Baa3 and BBB investment grade credit ratings from Moody's and Fitch. It has a safe net debt to EBITDA ratio of 4.9x, sitting below target range of 5x to 6x, and has a strong fixed charge coverage ratio 4.8x. FCPT currently yields 5.7% and the dividend is well-covered by an 82% payout ratio.

FCPT is in value range at the current price of $25.54 with a forward P/FFO of 14.3, sitting far below its historical P/FFO of 17.3, as shown below.

At the current price, FCPT would only need to deliver around 4% annual FFO/share growth in combination with the near-6% dividend yield. This forms a base case for potential 10% annual total returns, which I believe is reasonably achievable. Plus, any reversion to the mean valuation could result in market beating total returns.

Risks to Consider

Risks to MSDL include lower interest rates, which could pressure its floating rate investment yields. Unforeseen credit risks could also pose an issue, and could result in a higher non-accrual rate. Increased competition could also pressure investment yields.

Risks to FCPT include its portfolio concentration on restaurant properties. Tightening household budgets could result in less dining out by consumers. Higher interest rate could raise FCPT cost of debt and interest expense. Plus, new investment categories may be out of management's core field of expertise.

Investor Takeaway

Both MSDL and FCPT stand out as attractive high-yield opportunities for income investors seeking dependable cash flow in a volatile market environment. Each company generates stable income backed by hard assets and contracts.

MSDL offers exposure to a diversified portfolio of mostly first-lien loans to middle-market companies. It's supported by conservative underwriting, low non-accrual levels, and a strong relationship with Morgan Stanley's broader platform. It currently trades at a steep discount to its NAV with a 12% yield.

Meanwhile, FCPT delivers steady real estate income through long-term triple-net leases to well-known restaurant and service tenants. It maintains high occupancy, strong tenant rent coverage, and an investment-grade balance sheet. FCPT offers a 5.7% yield at a valuation below its historical average.

Together, these two names provide investors with a high income stream while providing a margin of safety due to their low valuations. For these reasons, they make solid 'Buys' for a diversified income portfolio.

data.title: AMD: Pullback After Blockbuster Deal Sets Up An Amazing Buy Point
data.description:
AMD: Game-Changing Partnership With Meta

Is it finally time for investors in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) to finally take the shackles off that have humbled and kept the semiconductor leader from benefiting from a higher valuation upgrade and keeping pace with Nvidia (NVDA)? While Nvidia is a $4.37 trillion behemoth, AMD’s market cap of just over $311 billion pales in (relative) comparison. No surprises then, that for AMD to demonstrate that it is capable of becoming a dominant standard in its own way against its much bigger, more illustrious, and arguably more successful “bigger brother." Then, we should expect the company to provide more incentives, so to speak, in convincing bigger companies, customers, and partners to give them a chance to demonstrate that Lisa Su means business, but they still need a platform, allowing them to scale and prove that they have the capability to not just keep Nvidia on its edge, but also become more influential and present in customer negotiations.

Is the AI infrastructure buildout dead? Nonsense. Far from it, we are not even close to the peak on the multi-year narrative that is expected to take us through to 2030. And please keep in mind that AMD has given us its longer-term prognosis, right? I don't think I need to point out to you the financial day numbers; you can find them for yourself. Nevertheless, a quick reminder: the outlook is for 35% revenue CAGR through the rest of the decade, and achieving a medium-term $20 EPS as well.

So it is definitely aggressive, but we expect nothing less from Lisa Su, right? Much has been said about whether AMD possesses the right technological stack, because Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem is going to become even stronger, right? Jensen Huang builds Nvidia on top of CUDA, whether it's for physical AI, AI factories, or even data centers across the hyperscaler or enterprise space. And for those of us who understand the immense power of network effects, I don't think I need to remind you how formidable this moat is, because when most developers are using your ecosystem, they aggregate even more value behind it, and underpinning the platform not only increases its dominance, but also helps keep your archrivals away from taking share away from you. In a recent commentary, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang brought us through his train of thought on how these line up for Nvidia:

It's a 5-layer cake, right, exactly. But because we've been working on it so long, it looks like everything is accelerated. But it's not true. It's because I did it one at a time, one domain at a time that all of the important domains in the world are now fully accelerated. And so the thing that we do on the supply chain side, our balance sheet is incredibly valuable because it provides security for our customers. On the upstream side, I'm cultivating new ecosystems for the future. All these AI natives that I'm investing in, the companies that we're partnering with, these are expanding, extending the CUDA ecosystem. 100% of everything that we do is on top of CUDA. Every investment that we've made is on top of CUDA. - Nvidia presents at Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026

Before we carry on, if you do not understand what this Nvidia five-layer cake is, Jensen Huang enunciated at the recent conference in Davos, as he emphasized why the company has become so entrenched across the technology sphere, as he shared how “AI [is not] a single technology but [is] a "five-layer cake," spanning energy, chips and computing infrastructure, cloud data centers, AI models, and, ultimately, the application layer.”

And I wanted to bring you through this preamble for us to better understand and appreciate just how challenging it has been for AMD in trying to convince the market, its customers, and its technological partners that it has what it takes to provide them more than a viable option other than being stuck in and locked into the CUDA ecosystem.

However, as we have learned from the AMD-OpenAI deal late last year, it also entailed AMD issuing them performance-based warrants that considered the installation capacity over time, and it seems like AMD has also gone to a similar playbook in executing the recent deal with Meta Platforms (META). So, I'm not that surprised that AMD clinched the deal while offering the warrants to Meta to strengthen their technological partnership.

AMD needed this deal. The market was already getting skeptical of OpenAI's narrative. Even though I upgraded AMD stock back to a Buy in my previous update, the fact that it was struggling for traction underscores just how uncertain the market has turned recently, since it doesn't have quite the level of market share and concentration as compared to Nvidia. So any kind of marquee arrangements that could rival the scale that the company nailed with OpenAI previously are going to make everyone more excited once more with AMD’s proposition.

In addition, these partnerships are not exactly new, right? While they may exist in a different form, Nvidia has also engaged in such strengthening propositions with their suppliers, their partners, and their customers. We also know that Nvidia provides data center lease guarantees and most recently participated in the $30 billion funding round to support a massive valuation of $840 billion for OpenAI, but the fact of the matter is, AMD is not going to print $200 billion in free cash flow anytime soon. Nvidia’s FCF projections are expected to almost hit the $230 billion mark by the end of FY2027, while AMD is expected to deliver just shy of the $7.4 billion mark by the end of this fiscal year. May I impress upon you the substantive disconnect?

So, I believe AMD is leveraging what it could deliver, while banking on the market's penchant for its higher potential for growth, as it attempts to gain more market share in merchant AI chips over that of Nvidia. But of course, unless your customers are truly convinced of your standards and your performance, then why should they switch, right? And if not, they will need something to incentivize their relationship to a higher level, in my opinion.

AMD Can Take Share With These Deals

The Magnificent Seven, arguably the main customers for all the biggest AI infrastructure spending through the rest of the decade, will see their operating cash flow numbers fall further this year. And if you consider next year, they need to spend more money to build out and compete even more aggressively against the rivals, which will necessitate them to optimize not just in terms of spending, but also the technology allowing them to acquire the best performance for watt per dollar.

However, if the technology has not been extensively tested, deployed at scale, or even convinced independent reviewers that they are better (because semianalysis also said AMD still has more work to do to close the gap with Nvidia in inference in a recent technical paper), then, it's hard to imagine how any disciplined, credible management of the Mag 7 will be more than willing to invest their balance sheet so aggressively without a sweetener that could move the needle, or shift the sentiments profoundly. Yes, I believe this is a sweetheart deal that AMD offered Meta, and I think I am not the only one who thought that way, as BofA analyst Vivek Arya opined to management when they announced that deal recently:

Lisa (AMD CEO), I had a more fundamental question, which is where is the economic value add here? You're giving away $30 billion roughly of value in your stock. And in return, you're getting about $30 billion, $35 billion of net income. So it seems like an even swap. So where is the value add here? So if I ask it in a different way, if the product is so good, why does AMD need to give up 10% of your equity? And is now every MI450 customer going to ask for this kind of a deal? - AMD Discusses Expanded AI Partnership with Meta and Multi-Year GPU Deployment Agreement

I thought that was an astute question from a sharp and experienced analyst, who spotted the economic value conundrum. Lisa Su’s reply mainly hovers around the “transformative” nature of the opportunity. I mean, I could understand that narrative. Because it means Meta could be building up to six gigawatts of capacity with AMD chips over several years, with one gigawatt expected to be deployed in 2026. So that's a good start, but of course the warrants vesting will also happen with the first GW deployed.

However, the market appears to be okay because the stock is still way up by 100% in the past one year, despite these pretty valid questions around the economic value of whether it truly benefits AMD or not. And if it is so good, why does AMD need to give away equity?

Well, I believe the reason for the market indicates that AMD could turn out to be a faster grower in the next few years. Based on Nvidia’s most recent earnings scorecard, I think it's fair to say that Jensen Huang delivered an outstanding result. Yet, I think the market is not looking at just CY2026. They are looking at whether AMD can sustain that multi-year 35% CAGR, and that will likely be the main difference that separates whether investors will continue to accord a premium to AMD shares over that of NVDA.

I'm of the opinion the market thinks that the potential for capital appreciation through more opportunities for share gains should offset these dilution worries. And since the vesting schedule will be in progressive phases, very similar to the one that they inked for OpenAI, the market hasn't been surprised to the downside. I think management did a good job in keeping the deal terms as close as possible, including the total gigawatt capacity.

Therefore, unless the execution falters unexpectedly, which could really happen, right? Supply chain factors going down to capacity, HBM, and advanced packaging, all these matters and constraints could feature more prominently, even as Nvidia reminds us that they have gotten it all sorted out, while AMD has indicated that they are not seeing any significant stress factors that could hold them back.

To lend further support, AMD also indicated that its CPU business is going through a much-welcomed revival that Lisa Su pointed out in her remarks. The market was possibly baking in a technological transition from CPU to GPU that could benefit Nvidia profusely at the expense of AMD. But it seems like such a one-way transition hasn't quite panned out (because we still need data center CPUs more than you think, yeah?). And, I think there is little hesitation that Intel (INTC) and AMD are the behemoths in this business, and this is something the market, I believe, will give the benefit of the doubt to its proven execution over the years, as AI inference becomes even more crucial as agentic AI proliferates across the enterprise:

And then frankly, we see just a tremendous demand for traditional compute as well. So if you look at the CPU cycle, we've always believed that the computing stack is heterogeneous and you're going to need CPUs and GPUs and FPGAs and all of these components. And that's really coming to fruition here in 2026. So a few months ago, we had our Financial Analyst Day. We put out an ambitious financial model to grow sort of 35% CAGR over sort of the next 3-, 4-, 5-year period. And I think as we look at the market dynamics, as we look at the product dynamics, I think we are very much on track to that and with an ambitious target of over $20 of earnings per share in that time frame. So lots to be excited about. - AMD Presents at Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026 Transcript

That's right. Pulling the plug on the data center CPU doesn't make sense, right? Even Intel is enjoying a mini revival on their own, breaking above the $50 level earlier this year, although it has subsided somewhat, in line with the recent weakness seen in the technology sector and semiconductor plays.

Buy Or Sell AMD Stock?

So if you ask me, this AMD outperformance has been a long time in the making, after suffering underperformance against Nvidia for several years, as Jensen Huang takes center stage. The reversal against Nvidia came early last year. And if you contemplate how the trend has been going up (follow my upward-pointing arrow annotated in the above chart), I think it's easy to understand why the market is banking on the faster-growing company through the rest of the decade.

AMD’s forward earnings multiple of 30x is valued above the 22x that marks Nvidia’s multiple right now. So sure, even if you were to say that the market is pricing in a premium, I agree with you. But so what? If the company can expect to gain more share and have better visibility to achieve its analyst day targets through this momentous partnership with the largest hyperscalers, big tech companies, and the AI pure plays, why shouldn't the market be more excited?

And look at the price action; when overlaying AMD over NVDA, the trend bias is consistently pointing to the upside. So even if we were to buy more AMD, we are following the dominant trend, so the risk-reward is strongly in our favor right now, especially since the market action is now retesting the key support levels as I indicated above along the blue line (50-week moving average)

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Grocery Outlet: No Turnaround In Sight
data.description:
Grocery Outlet Holding Corp. (GO) reported another weak quarter, sending the stock plummeting after earnings. The value grocer’s Q4 showed sales growth slightly below the company’s already weak guidance, and the 2026 outlook doesn’t suggest any improvement in upcoming quarters. Such weakness is pressuring earnings, and has led Grocery Outlet to close a number of underperforming locations. Grocery Outlet’s value perception is deteriorating.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous November 2025 article on the stock, titled “Grocery Outlet: In Need Of A Major Refresh.” The stock has since lost a further -49% of its value, meanwhile the S&P 500 (SP500) has returned 1%. The stock’s valuation now clearly reflects weak expectations.

Grocery Outlet Q4 Review

There weren’t any meaningful signals of a turnaround in Grocery Outlet’s Q4 report. I believe that the core focus remains on the company’s sales performance, which was increasingly concerning. Revenues came in at $1.22 billion, growing by 10.7% year-on-year due to an additional week in the fiscal quarter as well as Grocery Outlet’s continued store openings. Signaling the store performance underneath, Grocery Outlet’s comparable store sales declined by -0.8% due to a lower average transaction size, leading Grocery Outlet to slightly miss its 2025 guidance on comparable sales. Comparable store sales have continued to decelerate.

The topline result missed Wall Street’s already weak consensus expectation by $13 million, and the adjusted EPS also missed expectations slightly as a result.

For comparison, U.S. food inflation currently stands at 2.1%, in my opinion working as a benchmark for an acceptable comparable store sales growth rate. The Kroger Co. (KR) latest reported 2.4% comparable growth, Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) showed 5.8% U.S. comparable growth for January, and Albertsons Companies, Inc.’s (ACI) latest 2.4% growth in the metric has even caused market concerns.

A value grocer like Grocery Outlet should typically thrive in an economic setting where consumer confidence is low; the company was even clearly countercyclical during the 2008-2009 financial crisis. Instead, the Grocery Outlet brand has now clearly lost its value perception. The company also notes that competitors have increased promotional activity, further pressuring Grocery Outlet’s value proposition.

There are no clear improvements guided for 2026 as comparable sales only decelerated in January. Grocery Outlet expects total revenues of $4.60-4.72 billion for the year, reflecting very weak -2% to 0% comparable store sales growth. At the mid-point, comparable store sales are only decelerating further in upcoming quarters.

Earnings did improve in Q4, but only temporarily. Better inventory management more than offset pressure from increased promotional activity and markdowns, leading to a 20 basis point gross margin gain. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $68.0 million, growing by 18.8% year-on-year.

The improvement was achieved mostly due to one extra week in the fiscal quarter, though. Adjusted EBITDA is guided at $220-235 million for 2026, down by $26.8 million from 2025 at the mid-point. Fixed cost deleverage from lower comparable sales, and increased promotions, weigh significantly on the earnings outlook. The gross margin is guided at 29.7-30.0%, down from 30.3% in 2025. An earnings turnaround isn’t in sight.

The report leaves Grocery Outlet in a very uncertain position. Such an underperformance has pushed the company to decide to close 36 of the company’s 570 stores and a related distribution center during 2026, more than offsetting targeted 30-33 store openings for the year. Grocery Outlet clearly needs to reestablish its brand image as a value leader through improved merchandising, but regaining the value perception after losing it has so far turned out to be challenging. Investments in store refreshes also need to improve the brand’s perception. Continued weakness makes Grocery Outlet’s store opening plan increasingly questionable.

Without major reported improvements in the comparable store sales performance, I believe that investors shouldn’t expect much going forward.

Grocery Outlet’s Valuation Reflects Weak Expectations

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Grocery Outlet. I still estimate slightly elevated growth in the 2027-2029 period, reflecting Grocery Outlet’s store opening plan but underperforming comparable store sales underneath. I ultimately still estimate Grocery Outlet’s comparable store sales to stabilize at 1.5% over the long term while store openings halt.

I now estimate profitability using adjusted EBITDA. I expect the EBITDA margin to show a further decline to 4%, reflecting profitability pressure from lower comparable sales and higher promotions in upcoming years as well. The estimate doesn’t expect a rebound in Grocery Outlet’s performance.

In the cash flow conversion, I estimate half of EBITDA to be taxed at a 25% tax rate, that Grocery Outlet’s CapEx will gradually slow down from $170 million in 2026 to $100 million in CapEx by 2030, and that working capital growth and miscellaneous cash expenses cause a $20 million annual headwind on free cash flow.

The estimates put Grocery Outlet’s fair value at $7.4, suggesting 12% upside as of writing. Grocery Outlet’s valuation now clearly reflects depressed earnings expectations, mostly for good reason as the performance has continued to weaken. A turnaround could potentially create massive upside potential, but without improvements in sight, I believe that it’s too early to price in meaningful progress.

The fair value estimate is down from $13.9 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 6.46%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.26% interest rate, annualized from Q4 debt and interest expenses. I now assume a slightly higher 40% long-term debt-to-equity ratio as Grocery Outlet’s equity value has declined.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.11% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.61. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 7.16% and the WACC at 6.46%.

Takeaway

There isn’t a turnaround in sight yet. Grocery Outlet’s sales performance is only deepening despite increased promotions. The brand’s value perception has deteriorated. Store closures during 2026, and a concerning financial outlook for the year, weaken hopes of a recovery. As weak earnings are already priced in, and an unlikely turnaround could now create massive upside, I remain at a Hold rating for GO stock.
